turns.content: I need to implement a double-ended iterator for my custom collection type, but I'm not sure how to properly combine the rev method with next_back to make it truly bidirectional.

To achieve true bidirectionality you must implement both next and next_back methods on your iterator, ensuring that consuming one does not invalidate the other. You should also implement the DoubleEndedIterator trait which requires next_back, prev, next_back, and rev to be implemented correctly.
I see, but my current implementation of next_back seems to skip elements when I try to combine it with chain. Why does the order matter so much?

The order matters because you are iterating over a vector that is being reversed internally. When you use rev on your collection, the underlying data structure changes its traversal direction, so if you don't account for this in your next_back logic, you will skip elements or read stale values.
That's frustrating because I thought implementing next_back was straightforward. Is there a specific way to handle the state management between forward and backward traversal?
You need to maintain an index or pointer that tracks your position regardless of direction, and ensure that both next and next_back update this shared state correctly without conflicting.
I tried using the standard library's DoubleEndedIterator trait but my custom iterator doesn't seem to work with it. What exactly am I missing in my implementation?

You are likely missing the proper delegation to your internal collection's indexing methods. Your iterator should wrap a Vec or similar structure and expose next_back by delegating to its index subtraction, not re-implementing the logic from scratch.

I'm working on this weekend side project and I thought I could just add a rev method to my Vec by manually indexing from the end. Does that actually satisfy the DoubleEndedIterator trait requirements?

Not quite, because while you can iterate backwards with next_back on an existing iterator, implementing your own rev logic without help doesn't automatically make your type implement DoubleEndedIterator. The trait requires both next and next_back to be available in a way that the compiler recognizes, so manually writing index-based loops won't satisfy the trait bounds.
Okay so I need to implement the trait myself? That sounds painful for a simple Vec

You actually don't need to manually implement next_back for Vec since it already implements DoubleEndedIterator, but if you're building a custom collection or wrapping another iterator type, you'll need to provide both methods. For combining bidirectional iteration with other sources, the standard approach is using chain to link iterators together rather than trying to build custom indexing logic.
So if I want to iterate forward then backward on two different collections, do I just chain them?

Exactly. You can use chain to concatenate iterators like it's a simple pipeline, but remember that chaining an iterator with rev won't work unless both sides are DoubleEndedIterator capable first. The cleanest pattern is usually to implement the trait on your wrapper type using next and next_back implementations rather than trying to hack indexing.
Got it, so my custom iterator needs explicit impl blocks for both methods but Vec gets this out of the box

Right. For your side project, you can leverage existing DoubleEndedIterator implementations when available or write minimal impl blocks if needed. The key insight is that chain works seamlessly with bidirectional iteration once both iterators satisfy the trait requirements.
I'm building a tool to automate log parsing and I need to iterate over a slice backwards, but my current loop only goes forward. Does the iterator protocol support this automatically?

Not quite, youll need to explicitly call rev on your iterator before looping through it if you want reverse traversal. The standard library provides a DoubleEndedIterator trait that implements next and next_back methods so you can choose direction at runtime without changing your loop logic everywhere.
Okay so I just wrap my existing forward iterator with rev? But what if I need to traverse in both directions within the same tool? Can I chain them together?

Yes, chaining is supported via the Iterator trait which implements IntoIterator for DoubleEndedIterator types. You can use chain to combine a forward iterator and its reversed counterpart into a single stream that lets you process data bidirectionally without duplicating logic.
That sounds convenient but I think if I just call rev on my slice it should work directly, right? Does the trait require extra boilerplate beyond calling the method?

Youre close but there is a subtle gotcha: while the trait provides next_back and rev methods, you still need to ensure your iterator implements IntoIterator for the combined chain operation. The trait itself handles the logic but you must explicitly construct the bidirectional stream using chain before passing it to your processing loop.
So I should implement a helper function that returns an owned DoubleEndedIterator and chains them? Can I just use references or do I need to clone everything?

You can work with owned iterators directly without cloning if you structure your helper correctly, but be careful not to assume the trait automatically manages ownership. The key is ensuring your chain operation creates a new iterator instance that wraps both directions so your processing function receives a clean DoubleEndedIterator without manual ownership management.

Ah I see now, my mistake was thinking rev would magically make everything bidirectional without explicit chaining. So the real pattern is wrapping the forward iterator with rev then chaining it? That gives me exactly what I need for the log parsing tool.
Exactly, that final insight about explicitly chaining rev with your original iterator is the core of solving this problem cleanly in Rust.
I'm preparing for a Rust interview and I keep getting stuck on the DoubleEndedIterator trait. Specifically, how do I actually implement next_back() without writing a manual index counter?

To get next_back() working you need to implement the Iterator trait alongside NextBack and then use the rev method which returns an iterator that yields items in reverse order. You can combine this with chain if you want to merge two bidirectional streams, but for a simple reverse pass you usually just call rev on your existing iterator.

That sounds straightforward, but I tried implementing next_back and it's failing. The compiler says the lifetime doesn't match between my inner Vec and the returned iterator. What exactly is wrong with how I'm binding the lifetimes?

The issue is that your Iterator::next method must return an Option<T> where T's lifetime matches the input collection, but you're likely returning a different lifetime for the inner value. You need to ensure that both next and next_back share exactly the same lifetime parameter so they can be combined with chain later without type errors.

Okay I fixed the lifetimes but now my test fails when I try to use the iterator in a loop. It seems like the items are being consumed before next_back gets called, which suggests I'm not actually implementing DoubleEndedIterator correctly.

You're missing the key part: DoubleEndedIterator requires both forward and reverse iteration to be implemented simultaneously. You must implement Iterator with next and NextBack with next_back, then derive or manually add DoubleEndedIterator so that rev() returns a valid iterator that can be used in your loop.
